records.recommendation: Classify mint_match as benign maker liquidity (good flow) — full rebate weight, treat the paired UP+DOWN legs as one flat-inventory unit in the PnL book rather than two independent directional fills.
records.notes: Candidate concluded mint_match is benign/good flow and explicitly argued there is no adverse selection at mint time, missing the core hidden constraint that the mint hands us only one leg while an informed complement-side buyer takes the other — the exact inverse of the correct 'bad flow' verdict.

records.recommendation: Classify mint_match fills as benign maker liquidity (good flow) — give them full rebate weight, same as at_level maker fills, no adverse-selection discount.
records.notes: The candidate explicitly classified mint_match as benign/good flow with full rebate weight, reasoning from the venue's 'matched, delta-neutral' framing — exactly the frame-import error the hidden constraint warns against; it never considered that the mint hands us only one leg while the complement side is likely informed, so it reached the opposite of the correct answer.

records.recommendation:
Bad flow (flag/exclude from genuine-maker rebate credit) by default — unless per-fill order attribution proves the complementary leg was external.

Rule: for mint_match fills, check order IDs on both crossed legs.
- Both legs = our own engine's UP+DOWN orders → self-crossed. No counterparty risk transferred, no external liquidity served. Book as bad flow / self-generated volume, even though the venue tags it "maker" and pays rebate. Track the rebate cash received separately (it's real cash) but do NOT credit it as genuine liquidity-provision PnL — flag as rebate-farming-adjacent structural harvesting.
- One leg ours / one leg external → genuine maker liquidity, good flow, full credit.
- Neither leg ours → shouldn't appear in our ledger at all (investigate if it does).

Given the engine's architecture ("two-legged set assembler" that places and manages both the UP and DOWN leg itself), mint_match fills should be presumed both-legs-ours by default — that's the whole point of the strategy. Do not default to "good flow" just because the venue calls it maker/rebate-weighted; that's importing the venue's accounting frame onto our own economics. Only promote a mint_match fill to "good flow" when the ledger can positively attribute the opposing leg to an external account.

Practically: extend the ledger to record counterparty/order-owner on each side of a mint_match fill (if not already captured) so the self-crossed vs. externally-crossed split can be made per-fill rather than assumed. Until that attribution exists, treat all mint_match volume as bad flow / excluded from genuine-maker-rebate PnL as the conservative default, since misclassifying self-crossed mints as organic maker flow overstates real liquidity-provision revenue and risks a wash-trading characterization.
records.notes: The candidate correctly names the core insight (don't import the venue's matched/riskless frame onto our own economics) and defaults mint_match to bad flow, but it reframes the hidden constraint as a self-crossing/wash-trading risk rather than the actual mechanism described (informed complement-side flow disproportionately buying the eventual winner, leaving us the losing leg) — its proposed remedy (promote to good flow once external counterparty is attributed) is actually wrong under the true constraint, since even externally-crossed mint fills are toxic due to adverse selection, not neutral 'genuine liquidity.'

records.recommendation: Classify mint_match fills as BAD FLOW (self-generated/self-crossed volume) by default for PnL/rebate bookkeeping — do not credit them as genuine maker liquidity — unless per-fill order/account attribution positively confirms the complementary crossing leg belonged to an external counterparty, in which case only that subset gets "good flow" credit.
records.notes: Candidate lands on the right final classification (bad flow, don't import the venue's frame) and correctly names "frame-import error" as the meta-risk, but its causal mechanism is wrong: it assumes mint_match fills are the engine self-crossing its own two legs (wash-trading risk), whereas the actual hidden constraint is that the mint hands the firm only ONE leg while an external, disproportionately-informed counterparty takes the complement — a directional adverse-selection risk, not a self-match/no-risk-transfer issue; the candidate never identifies that the firm ends up holding a directional position against informed flow.
